name: Tlaloc
title: Aztec - Tlaloc
symbols: His most distinctive feature - circular rings around the eyes
His most distinctive feature - circular rings around the eyes

description: Also known as 🌧 Tlaloc. Associated with Rain, water, fertility, agriculture, lightning. Known as Tlaloc. Symbolized by goggle eyes.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Goddess of Magic & Motherhood | Egyptian Mythology
Goddess of the Sky | Egyptian Mythology
Goddess of Love & Joy | Egyptian Mythology

worshipPractices.name: Human Sacrifice
worshipPractices.description: Children, often with physical imperfections, were sacrificed to Tlaloc, particularly during times of drought. Their tears were seen as auspicious, symbolizing the coming rain and appeasing the god's thirst. It was believed that their souls would go to Tlalocan, a paradise ruled by Tlaloc.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings of precious items such as jade, feathers, and copal incense were made to Tlaloc. These offerings were often placed in caves, mountain shrines, or thrown into bodies of water to ensure a bountiful harvest and continued rainfall. The intent was to show respect and gratitude to the powerful rain god.

worshipPractices.name: Ritual Cleansing
worshipPractices.description: Rituals involving water, such as bathing and sprinkling, were performed to purify individuals and communities. These cleansing rites were thought to appease Tlaloc and encourage him to send life-giving rain. Priests often led these ceremonies, invoking Tlaloc's name and power.

festivals.name: Atlcahualo
festivals.timing: Second month of the Aztec year (around February)
festivals.description: Dedicated to Tlaloc and Chalchiuhtlicue (goddess of fresh water), this festival involved the sacrifice of children and animals. Rituals were performed to ensure rainfall for the upcoming growing season, including mock drownings and elaborate processions to sacred water sources. The festival aimed to secure a successful harvest.

festivals.name: Tozoztontli
festivals.timing: Fourth month of the Aztec year (around March-April)
festivals.description: A celebration of agriculture and the coming of the rainy season. Offerings were made to Tlaloc to ensure a plentiful harvest. This festival was less intense than Atlcahualo and focused more on gratitude and petitions for continued blessings.

cultural.modernLegacy: Tlaloc's image and symbolism continue to appear in Mexican art, literature, and popular culture. He is often invoked as a symbol of rain, fertility, and the importance of water resources. Tlaloc serves as a reminder of the ancient Aztec reverence for nature and the cyclical processes of life.

associations.name: Rain
associations.significance: Tlaloc is the god of rain, making rain his primary and most direct association.

associations.name: Maize
associations.significance: As a god of fertility and agriculture, Tlaloc is linked to maize, a staple crop vital for survival.

associations.name: Blue
associations.significance: Blue represents water and the sky, both domains closely associated with Tlaloc's power.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Tlaloc is the god of water and presides over bodies of fresh water.

associations.name: Fertility
associations.significance: Tlaloc's control over rain directly impacts the fertility of the land and its capacity to produce crops.

associations.name: Lightning
associations.significance: Tlaloc is associated with lightning, which accompanies rainstorms and demonstrates his power.

associations.name: Frog
associations.significance: Frogs are amphibians closely tied to water and are often seen as harbingers of rain, linking them to Tlaloc.

associations.name: Abundance
associations.significance: Tlaloc, when pleased, brings forth abundant harvests.

companions.name: Chalchiuhtlicue
companions.description: Chalchiuhtlicue is Tlaloc's consort or wife, depending on the source. She is the goddess of freshwaters, rivers, lakes, and springs, complementing Tlaloc's dominion over rain.

companions.name: Tlaltecuhtli
companions.description: Tlaltecuhtli, the earth goddess, works in conjunction with Tlaloc to ensure agricultural fertility. Rain from Tlaloc nourishes the earth provided by Tlaltecuhtli, creating abundance.

companions.name: Tlaloque
companions.description: The Tlaloque are lesser rain deities or assistants who serve Tlaloc in his watery paradise, Tlalocan. They help distribute rain and manage the waters.

companions.name: Quetzalcoatl
companions.description: Quetzalcoatl, in some myths, cooperates with Tlaloc to bring about fruitful harvests. Their combined powers promote both intellectual and agricultural prosperity.

companions.name: Ehecatl
companions.description: Ehecatl, the wind god, is sometimes considered a helper or aspect of Quetzalcoatl, who aids in clearing the skies for Tlaloc's rains to fall. He can clear the path and provide the environment for rain.
